item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow this is a really good scp it's testing log is a bit long and the level 4 part is a bit short but it makes up with the story. The story works because it doesn't tell you right then and there about the twist and the bond between the doctor and scp 4408 is alot like a real person. So now for this great scp I give you 1 updoot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a mistake- Dr. Lawler returned alone, but they use they/them/theirs pronouns. I My first post has a more detailed explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey I’m not sure if this is a mistake or not but in the last part of the credentials it says “they returned” which would mean that SCP-4408-A left with DR. Lawler but I don’t think that was what you went because his memory from being in SCP-4408 was wiped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. I loved the interaction between the two characters, they were such great foils for each other. I liked the natural development of their relationship, from frosty co-workers to close friends. I got so invested in their relationship that I was genuinely saddened that Lawler’s memory was wiped the second time around. I’d love to know what happened in their second expedition, but I guess that having that available would take away somewhat from the overall message that Sutcliffe really, really doesn’t want to enter Foundation custody. Anyway, this was a lot of fun to read. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I picture Lawler as an aging male researcher who wasn't very good at his job. His "I only read the important documents" retorts are a defense mechanism to make himself sound smarter when he really isn't. In the meantime, he's regularly compared to his much smarter coworkers which can make for a lonely life.

Thus it's kind of fitting that he finds an SCP that's also scared of people but for different reasons.
There's a bond there. Maybe that's why the Foundation chose him instead of a D-class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was pleasantly surprised when this turned out to be a character piece. Also, it may just be me, but I kept sensing a romantic tension between Lawler and Sutcliffe.

Upvoted for great writing and great character dynamic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oof, I didn't even think of that. I might end up changing it. Thank you much :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome, man! Can't wait to see what other works you'll publish on the wiki!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, this went up! Great job.

I'm not sure whether or not to be irked by all the temperatures being in Farenheit. It makes sense for Sutcliffe I suppose, but Dr. Lawler is a more modern Foundation scientist who would presumably think in Celsius..

Still, a quibble. The core concept here is pulled off with aplomb and that's easily enough to secure my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my favourite SCP. Good work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woof, this is a LONG one, but it works well. The dialogue was fairly good and the characters felt much more human, er, reality bender, for it. I liked this skip! Good job!! +1

item.title: Re: I enjoyed this one
item.content:encoded: Thank you much! Honestly, I was debating making it into a tale halfway through, since I was concerned it was too much "story."

item.title: I enjoyed this one
item.content:encoded:
I’ve always loved SCP articles that delve into interactions between people, and not just spooky monsters. Keep up the good work.

+1

item.title: Re: Clinical Tone
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I appreciate the feedback. If you could point out specific areas where the pronouns weren't clear, it would be a big help!

item.title: Clinical Tone
item.content:encoded: This is a really good idea, and I really like the dialogue. It makes the article go from good to great. But I think that the writing could use some work. You use some vague pronouns, and there is a clinical tone problem. If you could fix it, it'd probably be even better.

item.title: It's finally done!
item.content:encoded:
This one was one that I couldn't have done without the lovely critique and support of: XaiviarNightwing, athenodora, Azmoeth Jikandia, Meserach2, darkstuff, and ladykatie. Especially thanks to XaiviarNightwing, who looked at this draft in multiple iterations!

+ Image CC Notice
- Image CC Notice

This image was originally posted to Flickr by Christopher.Michel at https://www.flickr.com/photos/50979393@N00/14692694197. It was reviewed on 3 December 2014 by FlickreviewR and was confirmed to be licensed under the terms of the cc-by-2.0. Retrieved from: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Remote._-antarctica_(14692694197).jpg

+ Extras
- Extras

Adding this part since I beat my record! (…though it wasn't a hard record to beat…) And I kinda like talking. Don't take any of what's in here as 'canon' if you don't like it.

  • I was able to figure out which gender my critters were from how they referred to Dr. Lawler with a good degree of accuracy. Before I figured out what Sutcliffe was meant to be, I intended to make both Lawler and Sutcliffe gender-indeterminate (A la 'Frisk' from Undertale). Sutcliffe had to have a gender of some sort, given that I needed to refer to Wellington using a pronoun of some sort. I already headcanoned (Is that a thing if you're the author?) Sutcliffe as a guy, and since he's from the 30s Foundation he most likely he would have been, anyways. Officially, Dr. Lawler is nonbinary, using they/them/theirs in the two cases where I actually use a pronoun for them. I was debating switching them over to xe/xir/xirs to make it more blatant (And I'm still debating it), but I kind of like the ability for the audience to drop into the shoes of Lawler to that extent a bit more. It's not really noticeable to the reader, but amusing as an author.
    • Not criticizing you if you referred to Lawler as he or she- I actually had to reread this paragraph to make sure I didn't refer to Lawler as 'she' myself. I deliberately obfuscated their gender to achieve the 'Frisk' effect.
  • A more cynical look is that Sutcliffe isn't Wellington's ghost/astral projection/soulself/etc. That, in fact, the first person to discover SCP-4408 was a Foundation agent. The SCP reacted by creating what it thought was an uninhabitable environment, once it discovered what Wellington's intent was. It didn't kill the civilian because Weber was just an ordinary gal- no ill intents towards the anomalous. Additionally, it needed to figure out a way to handle things if another Foundation member came in. Thus, it created the "Sutcliffe" persona based off of what it knew from Wellington and Weber- perhaps Sutcliffe was the name of Wellington or Weber's boyfriend or something. Or maybe the SCP just really liked the name Sutcliffe. And perhaps we can take a closer look at the Foundation, and see that behind the scenes, they were going to send in Lawler, who wasn't so good at the "work" portion of their job but was more specialized than the other people the Foundation had sent through. There might have been errors in the data that the SCP made and the Foundation was catching on to. The SCP knew that Lawler was catching on, and reasoned that Lawler or another researcher might find a way around the wipe- it could be as simple as sending in someone with a supply of mnestics. Thus, the SCP decided to take a risk and go all in on the Sutcliffe persona, reasoning that if the Foundation saw the SCP as being on "their side," then they wouldn't be watching as closely or acting harshly.
  • It probably makes for a better story to take Sutcliffe at face value- but he did get caught out on a lie, even after Lawler discovered the truth.
  • I'm glad I was able to improve my writing this much. Honestly, I think the "How to Write an SCP" page needs a caveat- though it's true you shouldn't try to post an SCP right away, you should definitely start writing and drafting them right away. Like, I was concerned I was going to start annoying the forum crit team with the number of drafts I would put into the forums- I think I spaced them out well enough. But hey- there's only one way to get better. I'm happy with a double-digit score, especially this quickly. I don't want to sound like I'm tooting my own horn here- even if you downvote this, you gotta admit it's objectively better than what I did in SCP-3862.

Edit: Woo! 100!
